response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting mean validation accuracy and standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration. Variant_03, which omits the proposed component, serves as our baseline and achieves an accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6%. The performance of the other nine variants, each incorporating different configurations of the proposed method, is summarized below.

Most variants underperformed relative to the baseline. Specifically, variant_01 achieved 67.2 ± 0.8%, variant_02 reached 66.9 ± 0.7%, variant_04 obtained 65.1 ± 0.9%, variant_05 scored 66.3 ± 1.0%, variant_06 yielded 64.7 ± 0.7%, variant_07 achieved 65.8 ± 0.8%, variant_09 reached 66.5 ± 0.7%, and variant_10 scored 65.9 ± 0.8%. These results indicate that the majority of configurations incorporating the proposed component do not surpass the performance of the baseline; in fact, they show a consistent decrease in accuracy ranging from -0.6 to -3.1 percentage points.

Notably, only variant_08 demonstrated a substantial improvement over the baseline, achieving a validation accuracy of 70.4 ± 0.5%. This represents an absolute gain of +2.6 percentage points relative to the baseline, with a slightly reduced standard deviation, suggesting both improved performance and stability. No other variant exceeded the baseline, highlighting the critical importance of the specific configuration used in variant_08 for realizing the benefits of our method.

In summary, while most tested variants did not yield gains over the baseline, variant_08 stands out as the sole configuration to provide a meaningful improvement. These results underscore the sensitivity of our method’s effectiveness to its configuration and suggest that further investigation into the factors enabling the success of variant_08 is warranted.
